one_line_takeaway: A maximal-coupling green/red-list watermark that is provably unbiased yet stochastic, recast robust detection as sparse-mixture testing (sum test / higher criticism with explicit p+q=1/2 and 2p+q=1 boundaries), and empirically matches or beats Gumbel-max, soft green/red, and DiPmark on the detectability-vs-distortion trade-off while requiring fewer user edits.
key_finding: The scheme is empirically unbiased yet competitively detectable. On FinQA k=2 the method reaches TPR 0.975 clean and 0.975 under substitution, beating the soft green/red-list baseline (0.655 / 0.350) and DiPmark (0.925 / 0.815), while achieving S-BERT similarity (0.7963) comparable to those stochastic baselines and higher than Gumbel-max (0.7682). Gumbel-max remains strong on raw TPR but is the most repetitive decoder (18.61% repeated k-grams vs 16.77–17.80% for others on FinQA k=2) and more distorting. In the speculative-decoding (targeted-refinement) setup the method needs far fewer edits — average rejection rate 11.48% vs Gumbel-max's 20.97% (FinQA k=2) — at comparable TPR, implying less user editing effort. Between the two detectors, the SUM test beats HC at the text lengths tested (~300–370 tokens), e.g. TPR 0.975 vs 0.92 on FinQA k=2; HC is expected to overtake only for much longer texts (>10^3 tokens), matching the finite-sample HC slow-convergence caveat.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new UNBIASED variant of the green/red-list watermark for LLMs built on maximal coupling. Prior green/red-list schemes (hard/soft) distort the next-token-prediction (NTP) distribution, while the Gumbel-max scheme is unbiased in theory but uses a deterministic decoder that degrades text quality in practice. The authors' own contribution reconciles the two: given the NTP distribution Pt and a pseudorandom green list Gt, they form the 'hard' distribution Qt (mass restricted to green tokens) and then sample via maximal coupling using a uniform ζt — sampling from the overlap min(Pt,Qt) when ζt<=Pt,Gt and from the excess max(0,Pt-Qt) otherwise. This yields a provably unbiased STOCHASTIC decoder (Lemma 2.2/3.1): the marginal token distribution is exactly Pt, yet ζt conditioned on the token being green is uniform on [0, Pt,Gt], a distributional shift the detector exploits. The second contribution is casting robust detection (where a user edits some tokens) as a SPARSE MIXTURE detection problem and deriving asymptotic detection boundaries for two detectors (sum test and higher criticism/HC), including HC achieving the optimal boundary without knowing Pt.
threat_model: Goal: distinguish machine-generated from human text to counter disinformation/fraud/AI-content misuse, matching policy motivation (US executive order, Dept. of Commerce watermarking). A three-party protocol (provider, user, detector): the provider shares a watermark key ξ=(green lists, ζ values) with the detector; the user may return either genuinely human text (null H0) or an edited version of watermarked text. Adversary/user capabilities modeled: (1) substitution attack — independently replace each token with prob 0.1, which decouples ζt from the green/red status and makes the watermark signal SPARSE; (2) paraphrasing attack — BERT-based context-aware lexical substitution at ratio 0.3; (3) 'targeted quality-improving edits' modeled via speculative decoding, where a larger target model corrects a watermarked draft model's tokens (a more realistic human-refinement analogue than i.i.d. random substitution). An additional practical constraint: the provider may withhold Pt from the detector (proprietary), so the likelihood-ratio test is unusable and a non-parametric detector (HC) is needed.
limitations: (1) Detection guarantees are asymptotic; HC converges slowly in finite samples, so the theoretically optimal detector (HC) is empirically inferior to the sum test on the ~300-token generations tested. (2) The clean theory assumes Pt,Gt is bounded away from 1, which the authors admit is unrealistic for LLMs (deterministic spans push green-mass to ~1); they reinterpret such tokens as effectively 'modified', softening the guarantee. (3) Robustness under the paraphrasing attack is weaker than under substitution and often below Gumbel-max — e.g. FinQA k=4 TPR para. is 0.675 (Ours) vs 0.925 (Gumbel-max). (4) Empirical scope is limited to Phi-3 (3.8B) and LLaMA families and two QA datasets (200 samples each); no larger models. (5) The soft green/red-list baseline is evaluated at a single distortion setting (δ=1) and DiPmark at α=0.45, so the quality/detectability trade-off curves of baselines are not swept. (6) Speculative-decoding comparison is only against Gumbel-max, not the other baselines.

datasets_used: ELI5 (long-form QA; 200 samples per Tu et al. 2023 selection)
Simulated sparse-mixture data (m green tokens swept 10^2 to 10^5, 2000 replications, alpha=0.01)
quantitative_results: FinQA k=2, Ours: TPR 0.975 clean, 0.975 substitution (TPR aug.), 0.84 paraphrase (TPR para.); S-BERT mean 0.7963
FinQA k=2 baselines: Gumbel-max TPR 0.95/0.945/0.93 (S-BERT 0.7682); soft green/red 0.655/0.350/0.280 (S-BERT 0.8167); DiPmark 0.925/0.815/0.700 (S-BERT 0.7975)
ELI5 k=4, Ours: TPR 1.000 clean, 0.955 substitution, 0.925 paraphrase; S-BERT mean 0.7185
Text repetition (FinQA k=2): Gumbel-max 18.61% repeated vs Green/red 16.77%, DiPmark 17.80%, Ours 17.03%
Speculative decoding avg. rejection rate (FinQA k=2): Ours 11.48% vs Gumbel-max 20.97% at TPR 0.2850 vs 0.3100; ELI5 k=4: Ours 16.47% vs 29.81%
Sum test vs HC+ (Table 4): FinQA k=2 TPR 0.975 vs 0.92, TPR aug. 0.87 vs 0.71 (~355 tokens); ELI5 k=4 TPR 1.0 vs 0.99
Theoretical detection boundaries: sum test separates when p+q<1/2; HC (and likelihood-ratio) separate when 2p+q<1 (equivalently q+2p<1); impossibility when 2p-r>1 (Thm 3.4)
baselines_compared: Gumbel-max / exponential minimal sampling watermark (Aaronson & Kirchner, 2023)
Soft green/red-list watermark (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023a) with delta=1
DiPmark distribution-preserving watermark (Wu et al., 2023) with alpha=0.45
Internal detector comparison: sum test vs higher criticism (HC+)
